coredata.dc:title: Straightforward and controllable synthesis of heteroatom-doped carbon dots and nanoporous carbons for surface-confined energy and chemical storage
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Sul Ki
Lee, Hyunjoo
Choi, Min Sung
Suh, Dong Hoon
Nakhanivej, Puritut
Park, Ho Seok
coredata.dc:description: The valorization of waste is of prime importance for sustainable chemistry and replacement of fossil fuel-derived counterparts. However, existing synthetic methods are critically hampered by complicated and uncontrollable chemistry arising from heterogeneous sources. Herein, we demonstrate a separation-free, straightforward, and controllable chemical approach for the valorization of lignocellulosic biomass by conversion to heteroatom-doped and undoped carbon dots (CDs) and nanoporous carbons (CNs) via a novel mechanism. The incorporation of heteroatoms such as phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), and nitrogen (N) into the CDs and steam-activated CNs (aCNs) leads to changes in the electronic and surface properties, and affords morphological control based on the doping agents, for application to CO2 capture and supercapacitors. Controlling the chemical composition and structure of the CDs results in shifts and variations in the splittings of the PL peaks and fluorescence wavelength owing to the distinct optoelectronic features. The P, N, and S-doped aCNs (P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN) have robust honeycomb, entirely exfoliated reed-like, and hollow isolated reed-like structures, respectively, with unique hierarchical porosity. N-aCN exhibits the highest CO2 capacity and regeneration capability owing to the existence of basic N-containing groups and the associated structural integrity. In terms of the supercapacitive performance, P-aCN achieves the highest capacitance owing to the pseudocapacitance of the P˭O bonds, whereas S-aCN leads to the best rate and cyclic capabilities owing to the unique porous structure and S-containing group. These distinctive energy and chemical storage behaviors of the CNs highlight the importance of controlling hierarchical structure and heteroatoms for improved ultracapacitive performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Valorization
Carbon dot
Nanoporous carbon
Heteroatom
Hierarchical structure

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the P, S, and N-CDs
Synthesis of the P, S, and N-aCNs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical procedure for the valorization of lignocellosic biomass into doped-CD (P-CD, S-CD and N-CD) and doped aCN (P-aCN, S-aCN and N-aCN) and photo images of CDs (100ml) and aCN (2g).

TEM images (insets are the diameter distribution of the corresponding CDs) of (a) CDs, (b) P-CDs, (c) S-CDs and (d) N-CDs. The PL spectra of (e) CDs, P-CDs, S-CDs and N-CDs at 360nm excitation wavelength and of (f) P-CDs, (g) S-CDs and (h) N-CDs under varied wavelengths from 300 to 460nm (inset: digital photo for the P-CDs, S-CDs and N-CDs with 365nm UV lamp). (i) O1s, (j) P2p, (k) S2p and (l) N1s XPS spectra of CDs, P-CDs, S-CDs and N-CDs, respectively.

Low and high SEM images of (a-b) P-aCN, (d-e) S-aCN and (g-h) N-aCN. High magnification TEM images of (c) P-aCN, (f) S-aCN and (i) N-aCN. (j) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms and (k) pore size distributions of P-aCN, S-aCN and N-aCN.
High resolution (a) P2p, (b) S2p and (c) N1s spectra of of P-aCN, S-aCN and N-aCN. Elemental mapping images of (d) P-aCN, (e) S-aCN and (f) N-aCN.

(a) CO2 adsorption test of aCN, P-aCN, S-aCN and N-aCN comparing to 3D RGO. (b) Temperature-dependant CO2-sorption isotherms at 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 and 70°C. (c) Cycle retention during 20 cycles of N-aCN.

(a) CV curves at a scan rate of 10mVs−1, (b) GCD curves at a current density of 0.5Ag-1, (c) Rate capabilities obtained from CV curves at the scan rates from 10 to 100mVs-1, (d) Nyquist plots in the frequency range from 106 to 10-2 Hz and (e) Cycle stabilities using GCD curves at a specific current 1Ag-1 of aCN, P-aCN, S-aCN and N-aCN.
Surface area, total pore volume and average pore diameter of aCN, P-aCN, S-aCN and N-aCN.
Atomic and mass percentage distribution of C, O, P, S and N atoms in CNs, aCN, P-aCN, S-aCN and N-aCN.

head.ce:title: Straightforward and controllable synthesis of heteroatom-doped carbon dots and nanoporous carbons for surface-confined energy and chemical storage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU), 2066 Seobu-ro, Jangan-gu, Suwon-si, Gyeonggi-do 440-746, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Clean Energy Center, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, 39-1 Hawolgok-dong, Sungbuk-gu, Seoul 136-791, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Clean Energy Center, Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The valorization of waste is of prime importance for sustainable chemistry and replacement of fossil fuel-derived counterparts. However, existing synthetic methods are critically hampered by complicated and uncontrollable chemistry arising from heterogeneous sources. Herein, we demonstrate a separation-free, straightforward, and controllable chemical approach for the valorization of lignocellulosic biomass by conversion to heteroatom-doped and undoped carbon dots (CDs) and nanoporous carbons (CNs) via a novel mechanism. The incorporation of heteroatoms such as phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), and nitrogen (N) into the CDs and steam-activated CNs (aCNs) leads to changes in the electronic and surface properties, and affords morphological control based on the doping agents, for application to CO2 capture and supercapacitors. Controlling the chemical composition and structure of the CDs results in shifts and variations in the splittings of the PL peaks and fluorescence wavelength owing to the distinct optoelectronic features. The P, N, and S-doped aCNs (P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN) have robust honeycomb, entirely exfoliated reed-like, and hollow isolated reed-like structures, respectively, with unique hierarchical porosity. N-aCN exhibits the highest CO2 capacity and regeneration capability owing to the existence of basic N-containing groups and the associated structural integrity. In terms of the supercapacitive performance, P-aCN achieves the highest capacitance owing to the pseudocapacitance of the P˭O bonds, whereas S-aCN leads to the best rate and cyclic capabilities owing to the unique porous structure and S-containing group. These distinctive energy and chemical storage behaviors of the CNs highlight the importance of controlling hierarchical structure and heteroatoms for improved ultracapacitive performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignocellulosic biomass consisting of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin is the most abundant renewable resource. The valorization of wood waste (e.g., conversion to higher-value materials) is very attractive from the perspectives of sustainable chemistry and material science because the resulting products are more renewable than their fossil-fuel-derived counterparts in the context of lower greenhouse gas emission and the minimization of toxic elements [1]. However, the valorization of lignocellulosic biomass is technologically challenging because it is very difficult to recover high-purity cellulose or lignin from a heterogeneous source in an economical manner [2]. Separation is a prerequisite for the synthesis of functional carbon nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs), nanoporous carbons (CNs), carbon nanosheets, carbon nanofibers, and carbon aerogels [3–10] from these sources, and thus, the valorization process becomes more expensive and complicated [2]. Even more challenging is the direct transformation of lignocellulosic biomass into controllable carbon nanomaterials without any pre-treatment, where the physical and chemical properties of the latter be tuned by changing the structure and composition [2]. Thus, previous studies on the synthesis of carbon nanomaterials have been performed using only pre-treated cellulose, whereas lignin has been largely ignored and treated as useless waste because of the high cost of chemical isolation [11].

Among the carbon nanomaterials derived from wood waste, CDs are regarded as functional materials for the replacement of inorganic quantum dots because of the variety and ease of surface modification methods that can be used to tune their physical and chemical properties, in addition to their features of non-toxicity, good water solubility, photostability, and low cost [12,13]. With their large surface area, low density, moderate electrical conductivity, chemical resistance, and the easy accessibility of ions and mass, biomass-derived CNs can be used in energy and chemical storage applications owing to the high carbon content of lignin and the similarity of its molecular structure to that of bituminous coal [8,14,15]. Despite the intensive efforts to further the development of CDs and CNs, the existing synthetic methods still suffer from challenges because of the complicated and tedious procedures required for separation to obtain high-purity biomass, as well as difficulties associated with mass production [16,17].

Hierarchical porosity, where pores of different scales (micro-, meso-, and macropores) are simultaneously present, is very important for achieving high specific capacity (i.e., the amount of stored charge or chemical per unit mass or volume of active material) in energy and chemical storage materials [18,19]. The theoretical capacity of porous materials for charge or chemical storage may be enhanced by decreasing the pore size to microscale dimensions for a specific mass and space, which is proportional to an available surface area for a surface-confined system. Under kinetically limited conditions at a given charging or flow rate, charge and mass transport must be sufficiently fast to minimize concentration polarization and to achieve the maximum utilization of stored sites [20]. Such a kinetic restriction can be resolved by creating three-dimensional (3D) interconnected meso- and macropores [21,22], but the surface area is concomitantly decreased, thereby lowering the theoretical capacity [23,24].

Along with the surface texture, the surface chemistry is also critical for determining the capacitive performance of surface-confined energy and chemical storage systems such as supercapacitors and CO2 capture materials [25–29]. The capacity of porous carbons can be improved by tuning the surface composition and bonding via modification of the chemical or surface properties [30,31] or by (electro)chemical reaction with the surface-active sites [32,33]. For instance, the incorporation of various heteroatoms onto the surface of porous carbons changes the electron density of the neighboring carbon atoms [34–37], resulting in improved electrical conductivity, surface wettability, and binding with a target ion [37–39]. Moreover, heteroatom-containing groups can exhibit pseudocapacitive behavior arising from the redox activity of electrochemically active configurations [40]. Thus, the introduction of heteroatoms onto the carbon lattice can enhance the specific capacitance of supercapacitor devices. Apart from the effect of heteroatoms on the redox reactivity, the incorporation of basic nitrogen groups into the carbon lattice ensures improvement of the capacity for CO2 capture [25–27].

Herein, we demonstrate the separation-free, straightforward, and controllable synthesis of heteroatom-incorporated CDs and CNs through a one-pot hydrothermal method, where two precursors are isolated from wood waste and are respectively converted into carbon nanomaterials in a simultaneous and massive manner. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the proposed chemical strategy simplifies the synthesis of carbon nanomaterials from waste while improving the value of the product. The morphologies and textural properties of the porous carbons vary significantly depending on types of heteroatom precursors and the reaction routes. The capacitive performance of the surface-confined systems for supercapacitors and CO2 capture is significantly enhanced by precise modification of the surface chemistry of the carbon materials by the incorporation of P, S, and N heteroatoms, as well as by the generation of hierarchical porous structures via steam activation and self-assembly.

First, 1g of a raw empty fruit bunch (EFB) was dispersed in 5ml of deionized (DI) water. The dispersion was heated in an autoclave at 200°C for 12h. After the hydrothermal process, the resulting products separated into a liquid at the top and a solid monolith at the bottom.

The upper liquid was identified as CDs. On the other hand, the solid monolith was washed with DI water and ethanol to remove impurities. The resulting monolith was freeze dried for 48h. These precipitated carbon nanomaterials are denoted as CN. The as-synthesized CN was heated to 800°C at a heating rate of 6.5°Cmin−1 with an N2 flow and then physically activated by injection of steam at a flow rate of 0.17mlh−1. The final products are denoted as steam-activated nanoporous carbon (aCN). Both of the CDs and aCNs can be massively prepared on a scale of 100ml and 2g, respectively, as shown in photo images of Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the P, S, and N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the P-doped CDs (P-CDs), 2g of phytic acid was added into the as-prepared dispersion for the hydrothermal process. After the hydrothermal method, the upper liquid consisted of P-CDs, and the solid monolith at the bottom consisted of P-CN. For the case of S-CDs and N-CDs, thionyl chloride and melamine instead of phytic acid were used as S and N precursors during the hydrothermal process, following the same condition as P-CDs. On the other hand, solid monoliths were denoted S-CN and N-CN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the P, S, and N-aCNs
ce:section.ce:para: The P-CDs and P-CNs were synthesized in a similar manner as the undoped samples. 1g of raw EFB was dispersed in a 5ml DI water. To get the P-CN, 2g of phytic acid was added to the prepared dispersion solution. For S-CN and N-CN, thionyl chloride and melamine instead of phytic acid were used. Undoped sample for control sample (CN) monolith was obtained without doping agent. The mixture was placed into an autoclave, which was heated at 200°C for 12h. After hydrothermal process, the solid monolith was washed with DI water and EtOH to remove impurities. After xerogel formation, the carbon monolith was freeze-dried for 48h. The synthesized CN, P-CN, S-CN and N-CN were heated to 800°C at heating rate of 6.5°Cmin−1, with an N2 flow and then, carbon samples were activated by injection of steam at flow rate of 0.17mlh−1. The final products are denoted by aCN, P-aCN, S-aCN and N-aCN.

The electrochemical behaviour of the as-prepared samples was tested in 1M KOH electrolyte in a three-electrode configuration. The working electrode was composed of 70wt% active materials, 20wt% polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), and 10wt% carbon black. The active materials with the loading of 0.6mgcm−2 were attached on the Ti substrate acting as a current collector. These electrodes were dried at 80°C for 12h. Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were used as the counter and reference electrode, respectively. The GCD data are obtained from a two-electrode symmetric configuration.

A solid-state 13C cross-polarization magic-angle-spinning (CP-MAS) NMR study was performed using a Bruker AVAN-CNE II+ 400MHz NMR system. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was measured for the identification of single component in the aqueous solution. For the product analyses, 0.1g of CDs was dissolved in 1g of deionized water. Aqueous solution was then filtered through 0.2 μm PTFE membrane for HPLC analysis. Aqueous solution was analyzed using HPLC (Younglin 9100) equipped with RI detector (YL9170) and column (Aminex HPX-87H column (Biorad)) which was maintained at 50°C. The mobile phase was a 5mM H2SO4 aqueous solution at a flow rate of 0.6mLmin−1. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected on a JEM-3010 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, 300kV). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy was performed using a V-730 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Jasco). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7000). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FEI Sirion model) equipped with an in-house high-stability Schottky emitter. Information about the pore structure was obtained using a gas analyser (Belsorp-Mini II, BEL Japan Inc.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using a Thermo MultiLab 2000 system with an Al Mgα X-ray source at 200W. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were obtained using a Rigaku D/max IIC (3kW) with a θ/θ goniometer equipped with a Cu Kα radiation generator. Raman spectroscopy was conducted using a SENTERRA Raman microscope (Bruker) with a 532-nm laser. The electrochemical characteristics of the S-CNs were evaluated using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves, and impedance spectra using a potentiostat/galvanostat (EC-Lab, France) at room temperature.

The hydrothermal process described herein is very useful for synthesizing both CDs and CNs. After the hydrothermal process, a solid monolith and aqueous dispersion were simultaneously produced in a one-pot reactor and were localized at the bottom and upper sides of the reactor, respectively. Solid-state 13C CP-MAS NMR spectra of the CDs and CNs were obtained and compared with those of pure cellulose and pure lignin (Fig. S1). The spectra of the CDs and pure cellulose both displayed the typical C2 to C6 signals of cellulose at 55–80 ppm, as marked by the two asterisks. Signals of aliphatic carbons were also detected at 20–50ppm and those of aromatic carbons (CAr-O, CAr-C, and CAr-H) at 110–160ppm, indicating the presence of some lignin components in the CDs [41,42]. Interestingly, a carbonyl peak (C˭O) with quite strong intensity was detected at 180 ppm, as marked by the sharp symbol; the signal of this carbon was not observed in the 13C CP-MAS NMR spectra of lignin and cellulose. Infrared spectral analysis revealed a consistent result: only the profile of the CDs showed the carbonyl peak at 1734 cm−1 (Fig. S2). This result indicates that during the hydrothermal reaction, the aliphatic alcohol was oxidized to a carbonyl group in the form of a ketone, aldehyde, or acid. To identify this single water-soluble component of the CDs, the solution was analyzed using HPLC. Numerous peaks were detected in the HPLC spectra, but only the formation of glucose could be confirmed (Fig. S3). The NMR spectrum of the CNs showed broad peaks in the range of 110–160 ppm that were mainly derived from the aromatic carbon of pure lignin [43]. In addition, the profile of the CNs showed no cellulosic carbon signals in the range of 65–80 ppm, which is consistent with that of pure lignin [41,42]. The sample was subjected to a reflux process and the UV–vis spectra were used to derive the percentage of total lignin associated with the CNs, which was calculated to be approximately 95% (Fig. S3). The separation efficiency can be further improved by optimizing the hydrothermal conditions. These results indicate that the CDs were derived from the depolymerization of cellulose and lignin, whereas the CNs were derived mainly from lignin in lignocellulosic biomass [42].

The proposed processes for synthesis of the CDs and CNs can be explained as follows. During the heat treatment at 200°C for 12h, hemicellulose and cellulose were mainly decomposed from the lignocelluloses and remained as a supernatant in the form of a dispersion. This dispersion in aqueous media is attributed to the colloidal stability of the CDs functionalized by surface oxygen-containing groups, as indicated by the C˭O peak in the NMR spectra [44,45]. The proposed mechanism of formation of the CDs derived from the top liquidus part is as follows: (1) the celluloses are hydrolyzed and then converted into glucose (as observed by HPLC) and other oligomers, subsequently generating hydrophilic oxygen functional groups such as C˭O and C-OH [44,45]. Under the same conditions, some of the weak components of lignin undergo degradation to form small molecules via hydrolysis, and these are dissolved in the aqueous phase. (2) These products react with some monomers and with each other to produce soluble polymers that are further converted into CDs in the consecutive processes of polymerization, nucleation, and growth of the carbonized structure [45,46]. On the other hand, the strong regions of lignin are converted into a thermally stable solid monolith at the bottom of the reactor as follows: (1) in the hydrothermal process, the initial reaction is very rapid (occurring in less than 1min), where lignin is separated from the lignocellulosic biomass. (2) Subsequently, at high temperature and pressure, the separated lignin molecules approach each other, leading to the eventual formation of long chain monolithic carbons via condensation and carbonization [47]. (3) The lignin-derived CNs are then activated by the injection of steam to improve their limited pore volume and surface area. During the steam-activation process, the H2O vapor can selectively eliminate most of the reactive carbon atoms in the hydrocarbons, thereby producing a nanoporous carbonaceous structure with high porosity and surface area [48].

The morphology of the CDs derived from the upper liquid was confirmed by TEM images ( Fig. 2a). The CDs exhibited a fairly spherical shape and a narrow size distribution of 1.6–3.0nm, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2a. This finding indicates that the hydrothermal process is effective for mass production of well-defined spherical CDs, as shown in the photographic image of the 100-ml bottle in Fig. 1. To investigate the optical properties of the CDs, the brown top liquid was irradiated under ultraviolet light at an excitation wavelength of 365nm. Bright green fluorescence was observed (inset of Fig. S4). As shown in the PL spectra of the top liquid, the CDs exhibited strong emission at approximately 500nm when excited at 400nm. This PL emission is associated with the π-π* and n-π* transitions of C˭C and C˭O. Analysis of the O1s XPS spectra also supports the existence of oxygen functional groups such as C˭O and C-OH on the surface of the CDs based on the peaks at 531.90 and 532.62eV, respectively (Fig. 2i). The C/O ratio of the CDs was estimated to be 0.87. These results are consistent with the observation of the C˭O peak in the 13C CP-MAS NMR spectra.

The proposed hydrothermal chemical process is useful for simultaneously separating lignocellulose and synthesizing CD and CN precursors, as well as improving the physical and chemical properties of the latter via heteroatom incorporation in the same reactor. To demonstrate the benefit of the developed synthetic methods, the improvement of the physical, chemical, and textural properties of the CDs and CNs by doping was demonstrated. As a 'proof of concept', phytic acid, thionyl chloride, and melamine (used as precursors for providing P, S, and N) were added to the same reactor without additional treatment. The P, S, or N atoms could be readily incorporated onto the surface of the CDs. The morphology of the P-CDs, S-CDs, and N-CDs were not significantly affected by P, S, and N incorporation (Fig. 2b–d). The size distribution of the P-CDs of 1.5–3.3nm was similar to that of the CDs. In contrast, the size distributions of the S-CDs and N-CDs (5.0–13.0nm and 2.8–5.2nm, respectively) clearly differed from that of the parent CDs. Fig. 2f−h show the PL spectra of the P-CDs, S-CDs, and N-CDs excited at wavelengths ranging from 300 to 460nm. The P-CDs displayed two emission peaks under excitation at 320, 340, 360, and 380nm because of the different electronic features. In contrast, similar to the parent CDs, the S-CDs and N-CDs exhibited a single emission peak, but the peak positions differed. Even at low excitation wavelength, the energy of the P-CDs was sufficiently strong to excite two types of emissive chromophores. To investigate the effect of P, S, and N incorporation on the optical properties of the CDs, the UV–vis spectra were acquired (Fig. S5). The as-prepared CDs were able to absorb UV light at approximately 280nm because of the π-π* transitions of the C˭C groups. These transitions were slightly red-shifted upon P doping and blue-shifted by N and S incorporation compared to that of the undoped CDs, indicating different optoelectronic structures of the doped CDs [49].

The PL spectra of the CDs and doped CDs under 360nm excitation are presented in Fig. 2e. The P-CDs exhibited a clearly red-shifted (by approximately 23nm) emission, whereas the S-CDs and N-CDs showed a blue-shifted (about 24 and 6nm, respectively) emission with respect to that of the undoped CDs at 465.5nm. These peak shifts are consistent with the UV–vis spectral data. The distinct PL emissions of the doped CDs were further supported by the digital photographs of the fluorescence under 365nm ultraviolet excitation (insets of Fig. 2f−h). The P-CDs exhibited bright green fluorescence with emission at long wavelength, whereas the S-CDs and N-CDs both exhibited blue fluorescence, emitting at a lower wavelength than the CDs (460nm). Although the S-CDs and N-CDs are larger than the CDs and P-CDs, they emitted at shorter wavelengths than the CDs and P-CDs. It is known that the P, S, and N heteroatoms can change the band structures and density-of-states of carbon nanomaterials, resulting in distinct PL emissions [49–51]. Consequently, the red- and blue-shifted emissions of the doped CDs are attributed to the different optoelectronic characteristics based on the heteroatom composition and configuration, as well as the size effect.

The control of the optoelectronic properties by incorporation of P, S, and N was further analyzed by using XPS, as shown in Fig. 2j−l. The peaks in the high-resolution P2p spectra centered at 132.90 and 133.70eV represent P-containing bonding configurations such as P–C and P˭O [40]. The ratio of C/O for the P-CDs was 1.97, which is higher than that of 0.87 for the CDs. Similarly, the S2p spectra of the S-CDs showed the characteristic sp2/3 and sp1/2 peaks of C-S-C at 163.60 and 164.55eV, respectively, with a C/O ratio of 3.48 [52]. In the case of the N-CDs, the characteristic pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N peaks were observed at 399.81, 400.79, and 401.58eV, respectively, with a C/O ratio of 2.62 [40]. That is, the content of P, S, and N atoms in the P-CDs, S-CDs, and N-CDs was approximately 10.1, 1.0, and 2.5%, respectively, indicating that the P, S, and N atoms were successfully incorporated into the carbon lattice. These results indicate that the optoelectronic properties of the CDs could be readily modified by the hydrothermal incorporation of various heteroatoms.

Along with the top dispersion of CDs, the CN precursor from the lignin was deposited at the bottom of the reactor in the form of a solid monolith. The morphologies of raw EFB, steam activated aCN, and P-, S-, and N-doped aCN (P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN) are shown in Fig. 3 (see Fig. S6). After hydrothermal treatment, raw EFB was converted into rigid and crosslinked solid monolith CN via condensation and carbonization (Fig. S6). The as-synthesized solid monolith could be further carbonized at high temperatures and activated by steam injection. The monolithic aCN samples exhibited unique honeycomb structures (Fig. S7), which originated from the intrinsic structure of the lignin-derived products and were generated during carbonization and steam activation. [48]. The chemical reactions occurring during steam activation are suggested as follow [53,54].

In the steam activation process, carbon atoms of CN react with steam (H2O) at high temperatures, results in producing CO, H2 and CO2. Since overall reaction is endothermic, high temperatures need to be maintained during an activation process. Particularly, the lignin-derived CNs contain highly reactive oxygen-functional groups prepared via hydrothermal process, which can act as activated defect sites for producing porosity by steam activation. The existence of oxygen-containing groups was confirmed as shown in Table 2 (Fig. S8). Activated carbon atoms of CNs binding with oxygen-functional groups are not enough stable to be easily etched by generating gases at that place, where internal pores are produced with preserving carbon skeleton. CO2 gases generating from reactions (1) and (4) also acted as an activating agent, and then are recycled to produce porous carbon through a reaction (3). The steam activation of lignin under specific conditions, where the samples are treated at a high temperature of 850°C with steam vapor, results in the generation of the nanoporous structure by etching the defect sites in the basal planes and edges. The proposed hydrothermal reaction for generating heteroatom-incorporated CDs could be extended to modification of the morphologies and surface properties of CNs. The low-magnification SEM images of the doped CNs showed macroporous carbon structures (Fig. 3a, d, and g). The high magnification SEM images revealed that after the steam-activation process, the carbon materials had many micro- and mesopores with diameters of less than 100nm (Fig. 3b, e and h). Unlike the S- and N-doped materials, the P-aCN sample showed a special honeycomb structure constructed through a distinct chemical route similar to that of aCN. Phytic acid played multiple roles of creating robust and cross-linked carbon networks, generating an enlarged porous structure, and incorporating P atoms. Lignin was chemically modified by phytic acid to generate functional groups, such as phosphoric acid, that interact via strong hydrogen bonds to form the cross-linked networks [55]. This network facilitated preservation of the robust honeycomb morphology of P-aCN, even under severe activation conditions. On the other hand, the S and N precursors interact with the lignin sheets in different modes, leading to quite different reed-shaped structures compared with that of P-aCN. Notably, S-aCN exhibited an entirely exfoliated reed-shaped morphology and N-aCN exhibited a hollow, isolated reed-like structure with an empty core. The S and N precursors are likely to disassemble the compact and rigid networks, where the reactive vapor etches the defects of the carbon basal planes and edges at high temperatures. The heteroatoms were successfully incorporated after steam activation, while preserving the distinct honeycomb or reed-shaped morphology. Fig. 3c, f, and i show the micropores with sizes smaller than 2nm (corresponding to the dark contrast regions). In addition, a partial graphitic lattice was observed for P-aCN and N-aCN with long strand structures, whereas S-aCN with an exfoliated reed morphology had a less crystalline structure.

To investigate the pore characteristics, nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were obtained, as shown in Fig. 3j and k. P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN showed typical mixed type I and II isotherms, indicating the presence of internal micropores and multilayer adsorption. At relative pressures below 0.1, the isotherms were steep due to the numerous micropores. At high relative pressure, the typical feature of multilayer adsorption on the weakly interacting external surface of the honeycomb or reed-shaped particles was observed. The mesoporosity of N-aCN and P-aCN was more prominent than that of aCN and S-aCN. The percentage mesoporosity for P-aCN and N-aCN was 23.64% and 22.91%, respectively, which are higher than those of aCN and S-aCN (12.93 and 6.48%, respectively). The presence of mesopores was confirmed by the H IV hysteresis loop, corresponding to a hierarchical structure of micro- and mesopores. These findings are also consistent with the pore size distributions (Fig. 3k). As discussed earlier, distinct porous structures originated from the different reaction routes based on the heteroatom precursors. Importantly, the specific surface areas and pore volumes of the non-activated samples (CN, P-CN, S-CN, and N-CN) increased dramatically from 3.5m2 g−1 and 0.013cm3 g−1 to 1274.4m2 g−1 and 0.698cm3 g−1 for aCN, 3.6m2 g−1 and 0.011cm3 g−1 to 1154.2m2 g−1 and 0.714cm3 g−1 for P-aCN, from 2.4m2 g−1 and 0.008cm3 g−1 to 1009.9m2 g−1 and 0.450cm3 g−1 for S-aCN, and from 9.5m2 g−1 and 0.032cm3 g−1 to 1050.9m2 g−1 and 0.630cm3 g−1 for N-aCN ( Table 1 and Fig. S9). Consequently, nanoporous carbons with distinct morphologies and porous structures of were generated based on the types of heteroatom precursors.

XPS was used to confirm the chemical identities and environments of the heteroatoms ( Fig. 4a–c). The XPS P2p spectrum of P-aCN showed main peaks at 132.19 and 133.72eV, attributable to P-C and P˭O bonding [40]. The observation of these peaks indicates that the P atoms bound to the surface of the nanoporous carbons were preserved even after generating pores via steam activation. Similarly, S-aCN exhibited the characteristic C-S-C bands at 163.76 and 164.90eV and those of S-O species at 164.12 and 165.34eV, thereby providing evidence for the successful synthesis of S-doped nanoporous carbon [56]. The N1s spectrum of N-aCN revealed predominant peaks of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and oxidized N at 398.40, 400.80, 401.37, and 403.10eV, respectively, confirming the presence of N atoms on the carbon surface [57]. The content of P, S, and N atoms in P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN was approximately 1.97, 0.92, and 1.32%, respectively ( Table 2). The C/O ratios of P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN were 7.41, 15.27, and 4.87, all of which were lower than 19.92 for aCN. The data indicate that the content of oxygen atoms increased owing to the formation of oxidized species of the heteroatom-incorporated carbons such as P˭O, S‒O, and N-oxide. The existence and distribution of heteroatoms was further investigated by elemental mapping using the STEM images (Fig. 4d−f). The P, S, and N signals were detected throughout the porous carbon structures, indicating uniform distribution of these elements on the carbon surface.

The structures of P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN were further investigated by Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S10. The D band at 1330cm−1 is derived from disorder arising from defects or heteroatoms, whereas the G band at 1590cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of the sp2 graphitic carbon. After the activation process, the I
D
/I
G ratio for P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN was much higher than that for P-CN, S-CN, and N-CN, indicating that steam activation produces more defect sites of the heteroatom configurations on the basal plane or edge. This eventually forms the porous structure by etching the oxygen defects. On the other hand, a blue shift in the D and G bands was observed for the P-, S-, and N-doped carbon, in contrast with undoped carbon, which means that the electron-doping effects dampen the lattice vibration of the P, S, and N bonds (Fig. S10b) [40,58,59].

To evaluate the gas adsorption capabilities of P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN, their capacity for adsorption of CO2 was measured at room temperature in the pressure range of up to 1100kPa, as shown in Fig. 5. The CO2 adsorption capacities of P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN were determined as 5.76, 5.51, and 6.67mmolg−1, respectively, which are much higher than 3.44mmolg−1 for the 3D reduced graphene oxide (3D RGO) used as the control sample (Fig. 5a). Notably, the highest CO2 adsorption capacity was obtained with N-aCN, the value of which is much higher or comparable to those of most nanocarbons, such as activated carbon and graphene [57,60,61]. These results indicate that both the incorporation of nitrogen-containing groups and the hierarchical structure consisting of micro- and mesopores are effective for capturing CO2. The covalently tethered N-containing groups acted as Lewis-base sites for binding the acidic CO2 [25]. In other words, N configurations within the carbon matrix can increase the number of basic groups by changing the charge distribution of the carbon surface [27,62]. The effect of the sorption temperature on the capacity was investigated in the range of 20–70°C (Fig. 5b). The amount of CO2 adsorbed by N-aCN declined to 54% of the highest value when the temperature was increased to 70°C. This negative proportionality between the capacity and temperature indicates the exothermic nature of the adsorption process, where the adsorption capacity decreases as the temperature increases [63]. The favourable interaction between N-containing groups and CO2 was confirmed estimating an adsorption enthalpy (Qst) of N-aCN as shown in the following equation [63]:
Q
st
=
R
∂
ln
P
∂
(
1
/
T
)

Since Qst represents the average binding energy of an adsorbate molecule with the surface of the adsorbent, 13.81kJmol−1 of Qst for N-aCN adsorbent was indicative of a relatively strong physisorption between CO2 and N configuration.

For practical applications, in addition to high CO2 capacity, the regeneration capability is a crucial factor for assessing the performance of adsorbents. The N-aCN sample, having the highest CO2 adsorption capacity, was subjected to cyclic CO2 adsorption for twenty repetitions of the adsorption/desorption process at room temperature. N-aCN showed excellent cyclic retention with negligible capacity degradation (Fig. 5(c) and Fig. S12) and, which confirms its good regeneration capability and durability.

To assess the feasibility of the samples for energy storage applications, the electrochemical properties of P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN were characterized based on cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), as shown in Fig. 6. The capacitive performance of CN, S-CN, and N-CN was compared under the same conditions (Fig. S13). A three-electrode system was used with 1M KOH aqueous electrolyte. All the non-activated samples showed distorted curves and low capacitances, indicating highly resistive behavior. In contrast, the activated samples exhibited a clear rectangular shape, corresponding to typical capacitive behavior. The specific capacitances of aCN, P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN were 92.40, 298.43, 107.69, and 130.86Fg−1 at 10mV −1, respectively. These results indicate the following: (1) P, S, and N incorporation into the carbon materials increased the capacitance compared to that of undoped aCN. (2) Steam activation leads an increase in the effective surface area and easy ion accessibility to enhance the capacitive behavior. Moreover, the trend in the capacitances measured by CV was in good agreement with the results of the GCD analysis using a symmetric two-electrode configuration. In particular, P-aCN showed the highest capacitance of 298.43Fg−1. The superior performance of P-aCN is attributed to the surface redox pseudocapacitance of the P-containing groups, where the P˭O groups act as redox active sites, as previously demonstrated [40]. This pseudocapacitive reaction can be confirmed as shown in a broad peaks at potential window from − 0.25V to − 0.75V (Fig. 6(a)). The possible faradic redox reactions are as follows [64]. This pseudocapacitive reaction can be confirmed as shown in a broad peaks at potential window from − 0.25V to − 0.75V (Fig. 6 (a)). The possible faradic redox reactions are as follows [64].
(5) ‒P˭O + 2e- + 2H+ ↔ ‒(PH)‒OH

The rate capabilities of all samples were investigated at different scan rates from 10 to 100mVs−1. At the high rate of 100mVs−1, the capacitance retention of aCN was 49.57%; the calculated capacitance retentions of the doped aCNs were 49.27, 90.26, and 39.07% for P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN, respectively. Notably, S-aCN exhibited a good rate capability, which was two- or three-fold greater than those of the other samples owing to the fast charge transfer due to the fast charge transport due to the enhanced electronic conductivity by the thiophene functional groups. It can be explained that a lone electron pair among the sulfur atom p-orbital overlaps with π-orbital of the graphite sp2 hybridization to form an extended π-system with a filled valence band, resulting in delocalization of electrons along the conjugated backbones of carbon [65]. To further understand the outstanding rate capability of S-aCN, the Nyquist plots of aCN, P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN were compared in the frequency range of 106 to 10−2 Hz (Fig. 6d). The semicircle in the high- to mid-frequency region can be assigned to the charge transfer resistance (R
CT ) at the electrode/electrolyte interface. S-aCN showed an obviously small semicircle with a charge transfer resistance of 3.60 Ω, whereas aCN, P-aCN, and N-aCN produced large semicircles with high resistances of 69.38, 157.98, and 101.28 Ω, respectively. This indicates that the S-containing groups of S-aCN are associated with facilitated charge transfer kinetics.

For further practical application, cyclic tests of aCN, P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN were conducted by GCD at 1Ag−1 (Fig. 6e). After 6000 charge-discharge cycles, the calculated capacitance retentions were 94.73, 94.26, 98.26, and 94.16% for aCN, P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN, respectively. S-aCN showed the highest capacitance retention, whereas P-aCN and N-aCN exhibited good cyclic stability, comparable to that of aCN. Despite the higher capacitance and rate capability of S-aCN relative to those of aCN, the former possessed better cyclic stability due to its hierarchical inter-networked porous structure and strong heteroatom bonding configurations.

In summary, we demonstrated the mass production of CDs and CNs via a simple one-pot hydrothermal synthesis, wherein the optical, electronic, and electrochemical properties of the nanomaterials were further improved by heteroatom incorporation in the same reactor. Even without the use of complicated pretreatment processes and dangerous reagents, cellulose and lignin were simultaneously converted into CDs and CNs in a selective manner. The CDs and CNs were derived from cellulose and lignin, respectively, as components of lignocellulosic biomass, without any separation process and were present in the form of an aqueous dispersion and solid monolith at the top and bottom of the one-pot reactor, respectively. The success of this process was confirmed by solid-state 13C CP-MAS NMR, HPLC, and quantitative analyses. The CDs, which were synthesized through depolymerization by hydrolysis, polymerization, nucleation, and growth, exhibited a uniformly spherical shape with a narrow size distribution of 1.6–3.0nm. The P-CDs showed a red-shift of the PL emission, peak splitting, and bright green fluorescence, whereas the S-CDs, and N-CDs exhibited a blue-shift of the fluorescence with respect to the CDs. This change in the optoelectronic properties was attributed to the distinct optoelectronic structures arising from the existence of the P, S, and N bonding configurations. The aCNs, which were synthesized through depolymerization by hydrolysis, self-assembly, and steam activation, had a hierarchically porous honeycomb structure consisting of micro- and mesopores with a surface area and pore volume of 1154.4m2 g−1 and 0.71cm3 g−1, respectively, which are 330 times greater than those of the CNs. The incorporation of heteroatoms leads to morphological control based on the doping agents, where P-aCN, S-aCN, and N-aCN had robust honeycomb, entirely exfoliated reed-like, and hollow isolated reed-like structures, respectively, with hierarchical porosity comprising micro- and mesopores. N-aCN showed good adsorption capacity (6.67mmolg−1) for CO2 capture and could be regenerated over 20 cycles. In terms of the supercapacitive performance, P-aCN showed the highest capacitance of 298.43Fg−1, whereas S-aCN exhibited excellent rate and cyclic capabilities of 90.26 and 98.26%. Therefore, this chemical strategy offers a simple and useful route for the valorization of waste and provides important insight into the relationship between the structure or heteroatom-composition and the optical, surface, and electrochemical properties of carbon nanomaterials for many applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by both the Energy Technology Development Project (ETDP) funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry&Energy (20172410100150) and the Energy Efficiency&Resources program of the Korea of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) grant funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry&Energy (20152020105770), Republic of Korea.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ensm.2017.10.008.
